file_name: Arefi and Mousazadeh - 2025 - Hardware implementation of a quantized CNN model for early detection of skin cancer cells using hls4.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) implemented via the hls4ml tool for the early detection of skin cancer cells. The study trained models using the HAM10000 dataset with Keras and Qkeras frameworks, applying SMOTE data augmentation, and evaluated their accuracy for skin cancer classification.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' in the header. It presents original experimental work involving the implementation of quantized CNN models on FPGA using the hls4ml tool, training on the HAM10000 dataset, and reporting specific accuracy metrics (95.50% software, 94.90% hardware-emulated) and hardware resource consumption results.

title: Hardware implementation of a quantized CNN model for early detection of skin cancer cells using hls4ml tool
brief_summary: This study addresses the early detection of skin cancer by implementing quantized Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models on Field-Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs) using the hls4ml tool. The researchers utilized the HAM10000 dataset, which contains dermoscopic images of seven skin lesion classes, and applied SMOTE data augmentation to handle class imbalance. Multiple CNN architectures were trained in both floating-point (Keras) and quantized (Qkeras) formats to evaluate their performance and hardware resource consumption. The models were synthesized for FPGA implementation, allowing for a direct comparison of accuracy and efficiency between software and hardware versions. The study identified a specific quantized model that achieved a software validation accuracy of 95.50% and a hardware-emulated accuracy of 94.90%. These results demonstrate the feasibility of deploying efficient, low-cost AI-based skin cancer detection systems on portable FPGA devices.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset used for training and classification. Original class count: 1,113 images.

all_task_types.target: Seven distinct skin lesion classes (AKIEC, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL, NV, VASC) from the HAM10000 dataset.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'skin cancer classification' and 'early detection of skin cancer cells'. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (lesion classes) using CNN classifiers. Performance is evaluated using overall accuracy and confusion matrices for multiclass classification. The HAM10000 dataset contains seven classes of skin lesions.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions contributed by several institutions. It contains 10,015 images divided into seven classes. The study applied SMOTE data augmentation to handle class imbalance, resulting in 6,705 images per class for training.

use.training: Used for training nine different CNN models (both floating-point and quantized versions) after applying SMOTE data augmentation to balance the classes.
use.validation: Used for validation to compare accuracy and hardware resource consumption of the models.
use.other: The dataset was split into training and validation sets after augmentation. The paper notes that applying augmentation before splitting was done to ensure the minority class (DF) had sufficient samples for training, though this may inflate validation accuracy.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. The study compares model performance using accuracy metrics (software validation and hardware-emulated) and hardware resource consumption without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: The paper reports that Model 9 (8-bit) achieved the highest accuracy among the tested models. For Model 5, the most misclassified class was Actinic keratosis (AKIEC) with an incorrect prediction fraction of approximately 68%. For Model 9 (8-bit), the hardware-emulated confusion matrix and incorrect prediction fractions are provided in Figures 15 and 16, but specific per-class accuracy values are not explicitly listed in the text. The study notes that applying SMOTE augmentation before splitting the dataset might inflate validation accuracies compared to other studies.

architecture_used.pretraining: None (models were trained from scratch using Keras and Qkeras frameworks)

architecture_details.backbone: Custom architecture with 3 convolutional layers and 2 fully connected (dense) layers
architecture_details.layer_configuration: Convolutional layers use 3x3 kernels with stride 1. Filter counts per layer were varied across models (e.g., Model 9) but limited to powers of 2 (8, 16, 32, 64, 128) to ensure FPGA synthesis feasibility. Maximum filters/neurons capped at 128.
architecture_details.activation_functions: ReLU for convolutional layers; Softmax for the final classification layer
architecture_details.quantization: Quantization-aware training (QAT) using Qkeras. Tested with 6-bit and 8-bit fixed-point quantization for weights and activations.
architecture_details.bias: Convolutional layers used bias=False for floating-point models and bias=True for quantized models.

regularization.kernel_regularizer: L1 regularization with lambda 0.0001 for both convolution and dense layers

training_details.data_augmentation: SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) applied to handle class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset
training_details.hardware_environment: Google Colab with Intel Xeon CPU and Tesla K80 GPU

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected (Dense) layer followed by Softmax activation

preprocessing_techniques: SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) data augmentation applied to handle class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset before splitting into training and validation sets

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the HAM10000 dataset to handle class imbalance. The method creates synthetic data points by combining original data points within clusters. It was applied before splitting the dataset into training and validation sets to ensure the minority class (Dermatofibroma, originally 115 images) had sufficient samples. This resulted in all seven classes having an equal number of 6,705 images.

conclusions: The study demonstrates the feasibility of implementing quantized Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models for early skin cancer detection on Field-Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs) using the hls4ml tool.

A specific 8-bit quantized CNN model (Model 9) achieved a software validation accuracy of 95.50% and a hardware-emulated accuracy of 94.90%, making it the optimal model selected based on the trade-off between accuracy and hardware resource consumption.

Applying SMOTE data augmentation before splitting the dataset was critical for handling the class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset, particularly for the minority class (Dermatofibroma), and proved superior to standard augmentation techniques in terms of resulting accuracy.

The study acknowledges that applying SMOTE before dataset splitting may inflate validation accuracies compared to other studies, but considers comparisons within the study fair as all models shared identical training conditions.

Hardware synthesis limitations restricted the maximum number of filters and neurons to 128 and required filter counts to be powers of 2; larger models or higher filter counts resulted in synthesis failures with the current hls4ml version.

For skin cancer detection applications, latency is less critical than resource consumption, allowing the system to trade off inference speed for lower hardware resource usage, which supports the development of low-cost, portable medical devices.

The authors note that while the approach is applicable to other datasets and cancers provided synthesis limitations are not prohibitive, future work will focus on implementing this strategy for other cancer types (e.g., breast, lung, prostate) and investigating methods to synthesize larger models.
important_remarks: Data leakage risk: SMOTE augmentation was applied before splitting the dataset into training and validation sets, which the authors acknowledge may inflate validation accuracy compared to standard practices.
Dataset bias: The HAM10000 dataset is highly imbalanced (e.g., 115 samples for Dermatoﬁbroma vs. 6,705 for Melanocytic nevi), necessitating the use of SMOTE to handle minority classes.

Hardware synthesis limitations: The study was constrained by hls4ml synthesis limits, capping filters and neurons at 128 and requiring powers of 2, preventing the implementation of larger or more complex models.
Lack of external validation: The study relies solely on the HAM10000 dataset with internal validation; no external test set or independent clinical validation was performed.

Missing statistical significance: The paper reports accuracy metrics but does not provide p-values, confidence intervals, or formal statistical hypothesis testing to support the significance of performance differences.

Limited clinical scope: The study focuses on hardware efficiency and feasibility rather than clinical diagnostic performance, noting that latency is less critical than resource consumption for this application.

Model complexity constraints: The custom CNN architecture is limited to 3 convolutional layers and 2 fully connected layers, which may restrict feature extraction capabilities compared to deeper architectures like ResNet or Inception used in other studies.
